title: Black Tea on Wool
stainChemistry: Black tea tannins (high concentration, highly polar) bond strongly to wool's protein structure. Tea oxidizes faster than coffee—within hours it browns significantly. Wool requires cold treatment, dilute acid (brief), and extreme care. High tannin concentration in tea means treatment must be prompt and precise.
whyThisWorks: Immediate cold water flush removes bulk of fresh tea. Diluted acetic acid breaks remaining tannin bonds without weakening wool. Very brief peroxide (if needed) oxidizes residual color. Promptness is more critical with tea than other stains because oxidation happens fast.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Tea oxidizes FAST. Treat immediately. Ice water from back, blot hard. Concentric circles from edge inward. Never rub. Flip to front and continue. Work quickly—most of fresh tea will come out in the first 1-2 minutes. Delay past 2 minutes and oxidation accelerates.

Mix vinegar 1:1 with cold water. Sponge gently onto any remaining tan/brown. Acidity breaks tannin bonds. Tea tannins respond to acid very well. After 1-2 minutes, blot gently. Brown color should lighten noticeably.
Rinse completely in cold water. Acid residue will weaken wool if left. Work from back to front. Water should run absolutely clear.

spottingProtocol.agent: Dilute H₂O₂ (1.5%, cold water) if needed
spottingProtocol.instruction: If brown color remains, dilute 3% peroxide 1:1 with cold water. Sponge gently. Wool is protein—dilute is critical. After 1-2 minutes, blot. Tea stains typically respond to acid alone, so peroxide is usually unnecessary.
Final rinse in cold water. Gentle pressure only. Wool should feel clean.
Squeeze gently—never wring. Lay flat. No heat. Slow, even drying.

homeSolutions: FRESH: IMMEDIATE cold water blot from back. Most tea comes out if treated within 1-2 minutes.
ALREADY BROWN: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted) (1:1 diluted with cold water), 1-2 minutes, blot thoroughly, rinse.
PERSISTENT: Same treatment. If color remains, call professional—home options are exhausted.
materialWarnings: TREAT IMMEDIATELY—tea oxidizes fast and becomes harder to remove every hour
escalation: If tea stain on wool has already browned (oxidized) or if treatment doesn't fully remove color, call professional. Once oxidized, tannin removal becomes difficult and requires professional chemistry.

household.name: 28% Acetic Acid (diluted)
household.note: For tea tannin removal. Dilute 1:1 with cold water. Very effective on tea.

professional.name: StreeTAN
professional.note: For aged/oxidized tea stains on wool. Professional strength.

customerExplanation: Black tea contains high tannin levels that bond to wool. SPEED IS CRITICAL—tea oxidizes and browns within 1-2 hours. Cold water removes most fresh tea immediately. Diluted acid breaks remaining bonds. Wool is delicate, so we use dilute chemistry and never heat.
commonMistakes: Delaying treatment—tea oxidizes faster than any other common beverage
Using hot water—sets tannin AND causes felting
Not diluting acid—full-strength vinegar weakens wool
scienceNote: Black tea tannins are even more oxidation-prone than coffee tannins. Fresh black tea is light tan; within hours it becomes dark brown; by next day it's nearly black. This rapid oxidation is because tea tannins have extended conjugated systems (many double bonds) that readily accept oxygen. Each oxidation step darkens the color and makes the tannin less soluble. This is why immediate treatment is absolutely critical for tea on wool—waiting even 2-3 hours significantly reduces the chances of full removal.
callPro: If tea stain on wool has already browned or if it's older than 2-3 hours and treatment hasn't fully removed it, professional care is recommended. Once tea oxidizes on wool, removal becomes very difficult.
solventNote: Professional: IMMEDIATE cold treatment. Ice water flush, then diluted acetic acid (GAA 1:10 in cold water, max 2 minutes). For brown/aged tea, reducing bleach (StreePene sodium hydrosulfite 0.25-0.5%, cold, max 5 min) to reverse oxidation, then rinse thoroughly. If stain is days old, may be permanent. Temperature never exceeds 30°C. VDS post-treatment optional. Speed and promptness matter more with tea than any other beverage stain.
